note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old male presents with burning urination. He denies fever, flank pain, and testicular pain. No systemic symptoms reported. This presentation is consistent with male dysuria, raising concern for a urinary tract infection (UTI) in a male, which is complicated by definition, or other causes such as urethritis or prostatitis. Pregnancy status is not applicable.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No vital signs or laboratory data available at this time. No systemic symptoms reported.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): UTI in male (complicated by definition)  
  - Differential: urethritis (including gonorrhea/chlamydia), prostatitis, other causes of male dysuria  
  - Red flags considered: fever, flank pain, testicular pain—all denied

P:  
  - Disposition: Urgent care or primary care visit recommended for urinalysis, urine culture, and STI testing to guide therapy.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy may be considered after evaluation; pending test results, treatment can be tailored. Encourage hydration and symptom monitoring.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if fever, worsening pain, testicular swelling, or systemic symptoms develop.

Evidence (snippets used): None
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
